title: Galleries and Creative Studios Help
description: Portfolio, inquiries, proposals, and local visibility for galleries and studios. Taste in front, order in back.
html:
Industry help

Tech for the NYC gallery or studio that won’t have a marketing department.

A site that shows the work without flattening it. Inquiries that get sorted. Follow-up that happens. Built for a one-or-two-person studio, not a global gallery.

Gagosian has a whole team tracking every inquiry. You have a one-room gallery and taste. Keep the taste — we’ll build the tracking.

Published May 4, 2026

Page sections

01

Website needs

  • A public site that explains the work without flattening the taste.
  • Inquiry paths for commissions, visits, events, or retainers.
  • Proposal and follow-up systems that reduce manual copying.
      • 02

      Common tools

      Webflow, Squarespace, Airtable, Notion, Google Workspace, email, analytics.

      03

      Common misfit

      The work is strong in person. Online, nobody can tell who’s a buyer, who’s browsing, and who never got an answer.

      Fit map

      What usually has to line up for galleries and creative studios.

      The public site needs taste. The back office needs order. Who asked? What did they want? Who answered? A simple system holds both.

      We usually start with the intake path. What does the visitor want? What proof do they need? Who replies, and when? Light tools, clear answers.

      • Compare Webflow and Squarespace for Manhattan businesses
      • Build clearer portfolio and inquiry pages
      • Create a lightweight inquiry system
      • Start Here

        • Suggested first move

          Start where the day actually breaks.

          Sort the inquiries: buyer, press, visit, event. Each gets a clear next step.

          Preserve what staff and customers already understand.

title: Law Firms Help
description: Website, consult scheduling, intake, email on your own domain, referral tracking, and document flow for solo and small law firms across New York City.
html:
Industry help

Tech for the small NYC law firm up against practices with a whole IT department.

Website, consult scheduling, intake, email on your own domain, referral tracking, document flow — sized for the 2-to-15-attorney firm, not the 500-lawyer giant.

The big firms have an IT department. Your team has to keep client work moving while managing the technology too. We close that gap without the enterprise weight.

Call (646) 360-0318 Text us Email us Contact form Published July 12, 2026

Page sections

01

Website needs

  • A site that looks like your practice today, not 2012.
  • A consult request form someone actually answers, so a new matter never sits in voicemail.
  • Email on your own firm domain — an @gmail address makes a new client hesitate.
02

Common tools

Clio, WordPress, Squarespace, Calendly, Google Workspace, Outlook, Dropbox, QuickBooks.

03

Common misfit

Intake lives in voicemail, consults are booked by phone tag, referrals are tracked in memory, and documents scatter across email attachments.

Fit map

What usually has to line up for small law firms.

Trace one real client path: search, consult request, conflict check, engagement, matter updates, and the review or referral at the end. In a small firm every step has to work without a receptionist chasing it.

The first fix is often intake. We look at the consult request form, scheduling, the engagement step, email on the firm’s own domain, and how matter updates reach clients — so nothing depends on one person’s memory.

  • Make the site look like your practice today
  • Build an intake and referral workflow
  • Sort firm email and document storage

title: Medical and Wellness Help
description: Trust, booking, intake, and local visibility for small medical and wellness practices. Calm systems, clear handoffs.
html:
Industry help

Tech for the NYC practice fighting Mount Sinai and CityMD for patients.

Trust up front. Booking that works. Intake that doesn’t repeat itself. Built for a two-doctor practice, not a hospital system.

The hospital outposts have marketing teams and IT directors. The corner practice has neither. It just needs the same trust and the same easy booking — its size.

Published May 4, 2026

Page sections

01

Website needs

  • Trust-forward service pages and clear patient/client paths.
  • Booking and intake that reduce phone tag.
  • Careful handoffs, privacy-aware workflow, and local visibility.
      • 02

      Common tools

      Practice booking tools, Google profile, forms, Google Workspace or Microsoft 365, analytics.

      03

      Common misfit

      Scheduling lives one place. Forms another. Handoffs happen in the hallway. And the website says less than the door sign.

      Fit map

      What usually has to line up for medical and wellness practices.

      Patients check you out before they call — the site has to feel credible in one look. Behind it, the intake, reminders, and handoffs have to run without dropping anyone.

      We start by splitting two jobs: what the public sees, and what staff do. What does the site promise? What does the form collect? Where does the request land? Who moves next?

      • Strengthen Google visibility and Google profile basics
      • Clarify trust and service pages
      • Clean up intake and staff handoff

title: Professional Services Help
description: Websites, intake, proposals, and local visibility for small professional firms. Built so follow-up never depends on memory.
html:
Industry help

Tech for the NYC professional firm fighting the big-name consultancies for the same clients.

Clear service pages. Forms that ask the right questions. Proposals that don’t live in six inboxes. Built for a 5-person practice, not a 50-partner firm.

The big firms have whole departments for intake and follow-up. You don’t need the department. You need the system — sized for your practice.

Published May 4, 2026

Page sections

01

Website needs

  • Service pages that clarify expertise and fit.
  • Lead intake that qualifies the right prospects.
  • customer list or owner view visibility so follow-up does not depend on memory.
      • 02

      Common tools

      Webflow, Squarespace, HubSpot, Airtable, Notion, Google Workspace, Calendly, analytics.

      03

      Common misfit

      Your expertise is buried on page four. Leads come in half-baked. Proposals live in email, docs, and someone’s memory.

      Fit map

      What usually has to line up for professional service firms.

      A firm’s setup has one job: earn trust, then catch the right leads. The site shows what you do. The form asks the right questions. And nothing falls through the cracks after.

      We usually start with the leads. Who’s reaching out? What do they need? Where does it go next? Fix that path and the whole week gets calmer — no big software needed.

title: Restaurants and Bars Help
description: Website, POS, reservations, reviews, local search, and owner reporting systems for New York restaurants and bars running on tight margins.
html:
Industry help

Tech for the NYC restaurant fighting for tables when Sweetgreen opens down the block.

Website, POS, reservations, online ordering, reviews, owner reporting — sized for the 30-seat spot, not the 50-location chain.

Toast costs your local cafe what it costs a chain restaurant. We help NYC food & bev get the same caliber of POS + booking + ordering setup at a fraction of the bill.

Published May 4, 2026

Page sections

01

Website needs

  • Menu and service pages that load fast on mobile.
  • Reservation, ordering, phone, and map paths that are obvious.
  • Local search pages tied to neighborhood demand.
      • 02

      Common tools

      Toast, Square, Resy, Tock, Google profile, Instagram, email, analytics.

      03

      Common misfit

      Menus go stale, POS links sit apart from the website, reviews are unmanaged, and private-event leads disappear into inboxes.

      Fit map

      What usually has to line up for restaurants and bars.

      For restaurants and bars, the website is only one part of the operating chain. The menu, Google profile, reservation tool, POS link, private-event form, review path, and owner reporting all affect whether a customer turns into revenue or another loose thread.

      The first move is usually to trace one real customer path: search, menu, booking or order, payment, reminder, review, and follow-up. That shows which tools are worth keeping and which handoffs need to be connected or rebuilt.

title: Retail and E-commerce Help
description: Inventory, Shopify or Square, pickup, product pages, and local search for NYC shops. Everything agrees with the shelf.
html:
Industry help

Tech for the NYC shop competing with Amazon Prime for the same customer.

Your website, your shelf, and your register should agree with each other. Pickup that works. Emails people want. Built to fight Amazon’s convenience without Amazon’s money.

Amazon spends millions making buying easy. You don’t need millions. You need the same easy — sized for the storefront on your block.

Published May 4, 2026

Page sections

01

Website needs

  • Product and category pages that support local discovery.
  • Inventory, pickup, shipping, POS, and email connected cleanly.
  • Reporting that shows what is selling and where demand comes from.
      • 02

      Common tools

      Shopify, Square, Squarespace, Google Merchant, email platforms, POS, analytics.

      03

      Common misfit

      The website says in stock. The shelf says no. The pickup order got lost. The email list hasn’t been touched since March.

      Fit map

      What usually has to line up for retail and e-commerce businesses.

      One rule: what people see online has to match what’s in the shop. Products, prices, pickup, inventory — one truth, not two.

      A bigger platform usually isn’t the fix. Cleaner product pages, simpler pickup, one clear view of the day — that’s usually the fix.

      • Compare Shopify and Squarespace for NYC retail
      • Improve product and collection pages
      • Estimate software drag

title: Salons and Wellness Help
description: Booking, calendars, reviews, and rebooking for salons and wellness shops. Everything works together, and nobody double-books.
html:
Industry help

Tech for the NYC salon fighting Sephora for product sales and Drybar for clients.

Booking, deposits, reminders, reviews, rebooking. Sized for the chairs you have — not the franchise count Mindbody prices for.

The chain salons have booking down to a science. So can a three-chair shop. Same system, your size.

Published May 4, 2026

Page sections

01

Website needs

  • Clear services, pricing context, and booking paths.
  • Staff availability and repeat-client flow.
  • Review and local search support for neighborhood discovery.
      • 02

      Common tools

      Square Appointments, GlossGenius, Fresha, Mindbody, Momence, Google profile, email.

      03

      Common misfit

      Booking works. But the service pages, the schedules, the reviews, and the reminders don’t talk to each other. The owner reconciles it all by hand.

      Fit map

      What usually has to line up for salons and wellness teams.

      A salon’s system has a lot of moving parts: services, calendars, deposits, reminders, reviews, rebooking. They have to move together — without the owner playing referee.

      We usually start with booking. But booking depends on everything around it: the service pages, the Google profile, the reviews, the follow-up. We map it, then fix the weakest link.

      • Compare Square Appointments and GlossGenius
      • Clarify service pages and booking paths
      • Clean up intake and follow-up
